topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed on my monitor, a stark, unforgiving landscape of numbers. I scrolled down, my jaw tightening with each line that appeared. It was the quarterly compensation report, and the names swam before my eyes, each salary a tiny, sharp barb. Then I saw it: Sarah’s figure, juxtaposed against mine. A sudden, hot flush crept up my neck, making my palms sweat. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest, and stared blankly at the ceiling tiles. Every word I read felt like a deliberate slight, a quiet declaration of my own inadequacy. When Sarah finally walked past my cubicle, her easy smile radiating across the office, I managed a tight, almost painful nod, my gaze fixed rigidly on the keyboard, refusing to meet her eyes.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" The words left my mouth sounding brittle, like breaking glass. I watched Mark lean back in his chair, a slow, self-satisfied smirk spreading across his face as he casually referenced his latest bonus structure. A cold, heavy sensation settled in my stomach, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I gripped my pen so tightly my knuckles turned white. I wanted to slam my laptop shut, to physically remove myself from this sterile space, but I remained rooted. My eyes flickered toward Sarah, who was laughing with another team member nearby, and a sharp, unpleasant clenching occurred in my chest. I cleared my throat, the sound unnaturally loud in the quiet office, and immediately turned back to my screen, focusing intently on a meaningless data point, trying to smother the rising tide of resentment.

[story 3]

I kept my posture ramrod straight, a defensive wall erected between me and the reality unfolding on my screen. The numbers didn't lie; they were undeniable, a stark contrast between my hard work and Sarah's apparently effortless reward. A tremor ran through my hands as I reread the figures for the third time. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I picked up a stack of files, the paper feeling flimsy and insignificant in my grasp. Every movement felt exaggerated, deliberate, as if I were performing a ritual of suppressed fury. When Sarah approached my desk to ask a quick question, I met her gaze with a blankness that felt far more cutting than any accusation. My voice, when I finally spoke, was clipped and low, entirely devoid of warmth, forcing her to pause and wonder what had gone wrong.
